title: Microscopic universal theory of symmetry-enriched topological quantum spin liquids
abstract: An ultimate theory of a phase of matter should describe all its universal properties via quantities that are measurable numerically and experimentally. In this work, we present a microscopic universal theory of symmetry-enriched topological quantum spin liquids (TQSLs) in two spatial dimensions, which directly utilizes microscopically measurable quantities to describe the universal properties. This theory applies to generic TQSLs, which can be Abelian or non-Abelian, chiral or non-chiral. The symmetries are also general, which can include both internal and lattice symmetries, unitary and anti-unitary symmetries, and discrete and continuous symmetries. There can be spin-orbit coupling, the microscopic degrees of freedom may transform linearly or projectively under the symmetries, and the symmetries can permute anyons. The input of the theory is some microscopic states with anyons, operators that control the dynamics of anyons, and symmetry actions in the TQSL, and its output is a set of data characterizing the universal properties, whose underlying mathematical structure is a generalization of category theory. Based on this theory, we find an explicit bijective map between the universal data characterizing a TQSL with a symmetry described by a group $G$, where the symmetry actions may include both lattice and internal symmetries, and the corresponding universal data for a TQSL with only an internal symmetry group $G$, and thus establish a precise crystalline equivalence principle. We demonstrate our theory in symmetry-enriched TQSLs realized on quantum processors based on superconducting qubits, trapped ions, and Rydberg atoms, and in each example we verify the Lieb-Schultz-Mattis anomaly matching condition. Our theory provides a solid basis for identifying and manipulating symmetry-enriched TQSLs, which further paves the way for fault-tolerant quantum computation based on these systems.
summary: This work presents a microscopic universal theory to classify symmetry-enriched topological quantum spin liquids. It characterizes these phases using measurable quantities derived from anyon dynamics and symmetries, establishing the crystalline equivalence principle between lattice and internal symmetries. The framework is highly relevant for designing and understanding fault-tolerant quantum computation based on TQSLs.
main_problem: To develop a microscopic universal theory characterizing symmetry-enriched topological quantum spin liquids (SET TQSLs) by extracting their universal properties from physically measurable quantities.
main_result: The theory establishes the crystalline equivalence principle, providing an explicit bijective map between data for TQSLs with general symmetries and those with only internal symmetries.
method: The approach uses low-energy dynamics of anyons (motion and splitting operators) and symmetry actions to define universal data, which is organized using generalizations of category theory.
model_or_system: The focus is on two-dimensional (2+1)D TQSLs applicable to generic systems with complex symmetries (internal/lattice, unitary/anti-unitary). Demonstrations are planned for superconducting qubits, trapped ions, and Rydberg atoms.
key_observables: Anyon statistics, fusion rules, symmetry actions, moving/splitting operators ($M_{a}^{21}$, $(S_{bc}^{a,\mu})^{21}$), $\eta$-phases, and cohomology invariants.
important_parameters: The theory is concerned with the structure of universal data invariant under quasi-adiabatic continuation, linking lattice symmetries to internal ones.
main_assumptions: Assumes operators like $M$ and $S$ are unitary; approximations are made regarding exponentially decaying tails of operators when anyons are far apart.
figures_summary: Not specified in the notes, but mentions Figure 1 illustrating spacetime trajectories for moving operators ($M$) and braiding relations.
paper_structure: The paper systematically builds the theory by defining microscopic inputs (anyon states/operators), deriving universal data structures (generalizing category theory), establishing the crystalline equivalence principle, and verifying consistency via gauge transformations ($\eta', U'$).
why_interesting: It directly bridges abstract mathematical concepts (category theory) with concrete physical realizations in quantum hardware platforms like trapped ions and Rydberg atoms, providing testable predictions for exotic quantum states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 4. (a) The Av operators and Bp operators. (c) From a ground state, the state |m3, m4⟩can be created by applying the red m string operator, which is realized by applying σx operators to a string connecting the two vertices. When moving or splitting an anyon m from x3 to x4, we always choose the support of the string operator to be at the right side of the gray arrowed line pointing from x3 to x4. FIG. 5. The qubits are on the links of the kagome lattice, which are also sites of the ruby lattice. For the generators of the p6m symmetry, T1,2 are translations by 1 unit cell along the two directions as indicated, C6 is a 6-fold rotation around a plaquette with C2 6 denoted by C3 and C3 6 denoted by C2, and M is a reflection whose reflection axis if the double dashed line.
